subject: [PATCH 0/2] fuse: Fix possible memleak at startup with immediate teardown

reviews.author: Bernd Schubert
reviews.summary: Reviewer Bernd Schubert pointed out that his previously posted patch, which fixes a similar issue on the DDN branch, has not been applied yet and should be considered for this fix as well.
reviews.raw_body:
See my mail please it explains the history and shows the patch I had
posted to the list and which is not applied yet. The DDN branches have
it applied.

Thanks,
Bernd

reviews.author: Joanne Koong (author)
reviews.summary: Author asked for clarification on which specific patch or email the reviewer was referring to, indicating a need for more information before addressing any concerns.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Bernd,

Can you link to which mail you are referring to? Which patch are you
talking about?

Thanks,
Joanne

reviews.author: Bernd Schubert
reviews.summary: Reviewer requested clarification on which patch he was referring to, as his earlier mail may have been lost in a spam folder
reviews.raw_body:
The mail I had sent earlier today, a few hours after Horsts. Somehow I
have the bad feeling that half of my mails are going into a spam folder.
I hope you get this one.

Here is the link to the message-id
https://lore.kernel.org/all/3eabbc7b-010f-4d4c-9145-30d69fe1aa79@bsbernd.com/


Thanks,
Bernd

reviews.author: Horst Birthelmer
reviews.summary:
Horst Birthelmer noted that the issue of teardown running before fuse_abort_conn() can still occur even without the original patch's call to teardown, citing the possibility that queue_refs could be 0 by the time fuse_abort_conn() is called.

Horst Birthelmer noted that even if fuse_async_stop_queues() runs, there is still a window where queue_refs == 0, allowing fuse_abort_conn() to run and preventing the queues from being stopped, which can lead to accepting requests that will never be processed.

Reviewer Horst Birthelmer questioned the possibility of teardown running before fuse_abort_conn(), pointing out that teardown is only called by the abort path, and asked what ensures the async teardown work in fuse_uring_async_stop_queues() hasn't already executed when dropping the queue lock.
reviews.raw_body:
Yes and no ... ;-)
The original patch this whole discussion was started by had a call to
the teardown of the entries and I had that applied.
But even without that the problem can still occur that queue_refs is 0
by the time fuse_abort_conn() is called.

---

You understand correctly.
If the fuse_async_stop_queues() runs there is still a window where
we have queue_refs == 0. If in that window fuse_abort_conn() runs
we never actually stop the queues and we can accept requests which
will never be processed.

I have never seen this happen without the patch mentioned above,
but with that 'optimization' it happens regularly when you are able to
kill the fuse server and the application using the file system more or
less at the same time e.g. by an OOM event, when the kernel tries to
free resources.

To me this looks like nothing will stop this from happening, though,
but maybe I'm just not familiar enough with the uring code ...

---

Thanks,
Horst

reviews.author: Joanne Koong (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that the queue_refs == 0 problem still exists after applying her patchset, and confirmed that this is the same issue discussed in a prior email.
reviews.raw_body:
Thanks for the link! To summarize, what Horst was saying in this
paragraph then is that with this patchset applied, there's that
queue_refs == 0 problem (since queue refs is now dropped in the
fuse_uring_cancel() path) where abort then doesn't trigger since abort
checks queue_refs > 0 which is the same as the situation in [1].

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/4b5a8040-b62c-4d75-a474-70d0b4759461@bsbernd.com/

reviews.author: Joanne Koong (author)
reviews.summary: Author addressed Horst's concern that without the original patch, queue_refs could still be 0 by the time fuse_abort_conn() is called, and responded that she doesn't see how this case occurs except in a specific race condition described elsewhere in the thread. She asked for clarification on whether Horst was referring to a subsequent fuse_abort_conn() call.
reviews.raw_body:
Gotcha, thanks for clarifying.

Without the original patch, can queue_refs still be 0 by the time
fuse_abort_conn() is called? The only case where I see that is when
the sqes are in the middle of being registered but haven't grabbed the
queue ref yet, and then the abort logic runs (I am going to write more
about this race in a reply to Bernd's other message in this thread),
but other than that I don't see how without the original patch we run
into this case since teardown -> queue ref decrement only happens in
fuse_uring_stop_list_entries() which only is triggered on the abort
path. Are you talking about a subsequent fuse_abort_conn() call (the
one called from fuse_dev_release())?


Thanks,
Joanne

reviews.author: Horst Birthelmer
reviews.summary:
Horst Birthelmer noted that without the patch, the async teardown of the entry could run before fuse_abort_conn(), and provided a stack trace to illustrate this. He also confirmed that there is no other call to fuse_uring_stop_queues() than the one in Fuse_uring_abort().

Reviewer Horst Birthelmer questioned the possibility of teardown running before fuse_abort_conn(), pointing out that in upstream code, teardown is only called by the abort path and asked Joanne to clarify if she was testing a locally patched kernel.
reviews.raw_body:
To your first question if this can happen without the patch.
I have not seen it but the async teardown of the entry could run before
fuse_abort_conn() could it not?

With the patch the stack usually looks something like

Daemon Process Exit
  \u2514\u2500> io_uring_task_cancel()
      \u2514\u2500> io_uring_cancel_generic(true, NULL)
          \u2514\u2500> io_uring_try_cancel_requests(ctx, current, true)
              \u2514\u2500> io_uring_try_cancel_uring_cmd(ctx, task, true) 
                  \u2514\u2500> file->f_op->uring_cmd(cmd, IO_URING_F_CANCEL) 
                      \u2514\u2500> fuse_uring_cmd(cmd, IO_URING_F_CANCEL) 
                          \u2514\u2500> fuse_uring_cancel(cmd, issue_flags)
                              \u2514\u2500> fuse_uring_entry_teardown(ent, issue_flags) 

And this runs before fuse_abort_conn().

Without the patch fuse_uring_cancel() does not call fuse_uring_entry_teardown() directly.
You are completely right in the current version there is no other call to 
fuse_uring_stop_queues() than the one in Fuse_uring_abort().

So yes, without the modification there is no way queue_refs == 0 by the time
fuse_abort_conn() runs.

To your second question. 
Yes I meant fuse_dev_release().

---

Thanks,
Horst

2026-04-10.patch_summary: This patch addresses a potential memory leak and hang issue in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module. The problem arises from concurrent access to the queue references, leading to ent entries being leaked or causing the system to wait indefinitely for queue references to drop to zero. The patch ensures that the async teardown worker is running, guaranteeing that the ent entry will be properly torn down and preventing memory leaks and hangs.
